fact: December 2 is exactly 29 days before New Year's Eve - that's almost a whole month of waiting left!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when many squirrels start getting super chunky because they're eating their stored nuts like crazy to survive winter.
The sun sets before 5 PM in most northern cities on December 2, making it feel like bedtime when you're barely done with homework!
December 2 falls smack in the middle of Sagittarius season - the zodiac sign of the archer who shoots arrows at the stars.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, December 2 is when jacaranda trees explode with bright purple flowers that carpet the ground like nature's confetti.
This date sits exactly 19 days before the winter solstice in the north, which means daylight is shrinking by about 2 minutes every single day.
December 2 is when many northern lakes start forming their first thin ice sheets that look like glass but would crack if you stepped on them.
The moon phase on December 2 changes every year, but when it's a new moon on this date, the night sky becomes incredibly dark and perfect for seeing shooting stars.
In cold regions, December 2 is prime time for seeing your breath turn into little clouds - the air temperature drops just enough to make every exhale look like dragon smoke.
Many evergreen trees release their strongest pine scent around December 2 because the cold air traps the smell closer to the ground where we can smell it better.
December 2 marks the time when arctic foxes in the far north have grown their full winter coats, making them look like fluffy white snowballs with eyes.
The sunrise on December 2 happens so late in northern places like Alaska that kids might see the sun come up on their way to school instead of in the early morning.
